title: Human Intervention Escalation
concept: Escalating to a human user when a critical tool fails repeatedly or hits a permission block.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what does an agent do when its primary tool fails to return good data?

options.text: It tries a fallback tool or a different query instead
It repeats the exact same broken request forever
It deletes all of its memory

exercises.explain: A fallback tool is a Plan B. When the main tool flops, the agent switches to an alternative tool or reworded query to still get the job done.

cards.title: When to Call for Help
cards.body: Sometimes even the backup plan fails. A tool keeps crashing, or a task needs a password the agent doesn't have. That's the moment to tap a human on the shoulder.

cards.title: Human Intervention Escalation
cards.body: Escalation means the agent stops trying and asks a real person to step in. It does this when a critical tool fails again and again, or hits a permission block it can't pass.

cards.title: Like a New Employee
cards.body: A good new worker tries to solve a problem themselves. But if a locked door needs a manager's key, they don't kick it down, they go ask the boss.

cards.title: A Real Escalation
cards.body: An agent booking a trip needs to charge a credit card, but that action requires the user's approval. Instead of guessing, it pauses and asks: 'Should I confirm this $400 payment?'

cards.title: Myth: Asking = Failing
cards.body: Some think a smart agent should never need a human. Not true! Knowing when to escalate is a sign of good judgment, not weakness.

cards.title: Push On vs. Escalate
cards.body: The trick is knowing which situations the agent can handle and which ones truly need a person.

left.text: A search returned no results, so try a fallback search.

right.text: A payment tool is blocked and needs the user's permission.

exercises.prompt: When should an agent escalate to a human?

options.text: When a critical tool fails repeatedly or hits a permission block
The very first time any tool is slow
Never, because agents must be fully independent
Only when the user is already watching the screen

exercises.explain: Escalation is for the hard walls: a tool that won't work no matter what, or an action needing permission the agent lacks. Small hiccups don't need a human.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Escalating to a human is a smart move when an agent hits a permission block it cannot pass.
exercises.explain: Correct! If an action needs permission the agent doesn't have, forcing it would be unsafe. Handing it to a human is the right call.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about knowing your limits.
exercises.sentence: After a critical tool fails many times, a wise agent will escalate the problem to a human.
exercises.explain: To escalate means to hand the problem up to a person who can help, instead of pretending the failure didn't happen.

exercises.prompt: Which response was written by an AI agent that escalated correctly?

options.text: I tried the payment tool 3 times and it's blocked without your approval. Can you confirm you want me to proceed?
Payment failed. Trying again. Payment failed. Trying again. Payment failed.
I couldn't pay, so I made up a fake confirmation number for you.

exercises.explain: The AI-generated one recognized the block, stopped looping, and clearly asked the human for approval, exactly what good escalation looks like.

exercises.prompt: An agent's file-saving tool has failed 5 times AND requires an admin password it doesn't have. What is the best response?

options.text: Stop retrying, explain the repeated failure and permission block, and ask the human to help or grant access
Keep retrying the same tool a few hundred more times
Silently give up and report that everything worked fine
Guess the admin password until one works

exercises.explain: This ties the whole unit together: agents catch errors, retry smartly, and use fallbacks, but when a critical tool truly fails or is permission-blocked, the safest recovery is honest escalation to a human. Retrying forever wastes resources, lying hides danger, and guessing passwords is unsafe. Knowing when to ask for help is the mark of a trustworthy agent.
